summary: Dan Shapiro announces his advisory role at Highway One, a San Francisco-based hardware incubator backed by PCH, one of the world's largest manufacturing companies. He argues that hardware startups need specialized support because hardware is fundamentally different from software. Highway One's unique value proposition is direct access to PCH's manufacturing expertise and factories in Shenzhen, which would otherwise be inaccessible to startups. The program offers hands-on manufacturing experience, engineering talent, and a $20k investment for 3%-6% equity.
key_points: Hardware startups need specialized incubation because hardware development is fundamentally different from software
Highway One's key differentiator is its connection to PCH, giving startups access to world-class manufacturing infrastructure normally reserved for Fortune 500 companies
The program includes travel to Shenzhen for hands-on manufacturing experience with professional engineers
The investment terms are $20k for 3%-6% equity, a modest financial component relative to the manufacturing access
money_quotes: Because hardware's hard, and hardware's different.
It's not an accelerator for hardware startups, it's a hardware accelerator.
They are all about getting your designs rolling off assembly lines, fast.
As a startup you can't get access to these folks' manufacturing expertise or factories for love or money.
You'll actually travel to Shenzhen with them to go learn how to manufacture stuff, in situ, in volume, with the pros.
key_insight: The biggest barrier for hardware startups isn't funding or ideas but access to world-class manufacturing expertise, which purpose-built incubators backed by major manufacturers can uniquely provide.
